title: Tannin pre-mordant for plant fibres
subtitle: Preparing cotton and linen with tannin to improve natural dye uptake
excerpt: Plant fibres (cotton, linen, hemp) do not bond with natural dyes as readily as protein fibres (wool, silk). A tannin pre-mordant deposits tannin compounds into the cellulose fibre, which then hold alum and dye more effectively. Common tannin sources include oak galls, sumac leaves, black tea, and walnut hulls. The tannin step precedes the alum mordant step and is not a replacement for it.
sourceNotes: Tannin pre-mordanting for cellulose fibres is a standard natural dyeing practice documented in contemporary and historical textile literature.

glossaryTerms.definition: A group of plant-derived compounds that bond to fibre and act as an intermediary between cellulose and alum mordant. Tannin-rich plant sources used as pre-mordants include oak galls, sumac, black tea, and walnut hulls.
A plant-derived textile fibre, including cotton, linen, hemp, and nettle. Cellulose fibres require a tannin pre-mordant step before alum mordanting to achieve good dye uptake.

content.text: to accept alum and then dye. The tannin bath is worked at low heat to avoid discolouring the fibre permanently: high-tannin sources like oak galls and walnut can stain light-coloured fabric if overprocessed. Black tea is the mildest option and adds the least background colour. After the tannin step, the fibre is mordanted with alum as usual before dyeing.

items.name: Cotton or linen fabric or yarn, scoured and damp
Oak galls (10 g per 100 g fibre), or black tea (20 g per 100 g fibre)

content.text: Simmer the tannin source (oak galls, sumac, or tea bags) in water at 70 to 80°C for 45 minutes. Strain out the plant material. The liquid will be pale amber (tea), dark brown (oak gall or walnut), or yellow-green (sumac). A darker bath does not necessarily give a stronger mordant result; the tannin concentration matters more than the colour.

Wet out the fabric or yarn thoroughly before adding to the tannin bath. Enter the fibre into the bath at 50°C and raise to 70°C over 20 minutes. Hold at 70°C for 45 minutes, moving the fibre gently every 10 minutes to ensure even penetration. Do not let the bath boil.
Step 3: Rinse and proceed to alum

Remove the fibre from the tannin bath and rinse in warm water. Do not rinse vigorously: heavy rinsing removes some of the deposited tannin from the fibre. The fibre will be lightly coloured by the tannin. Proceed immediately to alum mordanting, or store damp in a sealed bag for up to 48 hours.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Two common problems with tannin pre-mordanting.

items.symptom: Finished dyed fabric has a persistent brown background tint
items.cause: Tannin source was too concentrated, or fibre was left in the tannin bath too long.
items.fix: Use black tea rather than oak gall for light-coloured fabrics. Reduce the tannin quantity and bath time. The brown tint from tea is less visible than from walnut or oak gall.

items.symptom: Dye uptake is still poor despite tannin treatment
items.cause: The alum mordant step was skipped or the alum quantity was too low.
items.fix: Tannin is a pre-mordant, not a mordant. It prepares the fibre for alum; it does not replace it. Follow the tannin step with a standard alum mordant at 15 to 20 percent WOF.
